title: Combination Calculator (nCr) — Choose r from n
shortDescription: Calculate the number of combinations nCr for choosing r items from n when order does not matter.
description: This combination calculator computes nCr = n! / (r!·(n−r)!), the number of ways to choose r items from a set of n when order does not matter. Enter n and r (with 0 ≤ r ≤ n) to get the result instantly for combinatorics and probability problems.
metaDescription: Free combination calculator (nCr). Find how many ways to choose r items from n when order does not matter, using nCr = n! / (r!·(n−r)!).

result.label: Combinations (nCr)
result.empty: Enter n and r to see the number of combinations.

sections.heading: What is a combination?
paragraphs: A combination counts how many distinct groups of r items you can choose from n items when the order of selection does not matter. Picking {A, B} is the same as picking {B, A}.
The count is written nCr or C(n, r) and is calculated with the formula nCr = n! / (r!·(n−r)!), valid whenever 0 ≤ r ≤ n.

sections.heading: Combinations vs. permutations
paragraphs: Use a combination when order is irrelevant, such as selecting a committee or a lottery draw. Use a permutation when order matters, such as ranking finishers in a race.
Because permutations count ordered arrangements, nPr is always greater than or equal to nCr: nPr = nCr · r!.

howTo.name: Enter the total count n
howTo.text: Type the total number of items n in the first field. It must be a non-negative whole number.

howTo.name: Enter the chosen count r
howTo.text: Type how many items r you want to choose. Make sure 0 ≤ r ≤ n.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The calculator shows nCr, the number of ways to choose r items from n ignoring order.

faq.a: nCr is the number of combinations: how many ways you can choose r items from n when order does not matter.

faq.q: What is the formula for nCr?
faq.a: nCr = n! / (r!·(n−r)!), where n! is the factorial of n. It is valid when 0 ≤ r ≤ n.

faq.q: What is the difference between a combination and a permutation?
faq.a: A combination ignores order, while a permutation counts ordered arrangements. nPr = nCr · r!.
Both equal 1. There is exactly one way to choose nothing and exactly one way to choose everything.

faq.q: Can r be greater than n?
faq.a: No. The formula requires 0 ≤ r ≤ n; you cannot choose more items than are available.
